# US Forest Service, Learning From Unintended Outcomes (LFUO/FLA/LR) — 2024 Implementation Guide (deep reference)

**Source:** US Forest Service, Risk Management Council & Office of Safety & Occupational Health (2024). *Learning From Unintended Outcomes and Learning Review Implementation Guide*. Revised April 2024. 98 pages. Hosted at https://lessonslearned-prod-media-bucket.s3.us-gov-west-1.amazonaws.com/2024-06/LFUO_2024.pdf with the canonical entry at the Wildland Fire Lessons Learned Center (http://lessons.wildfire.gov/library/facilitated-learning-analysis-fla-implementation-guide-2024). Licence: **Public domain in the United States** (17 USC §105 — federal-employee work).
**Scope:** open
**Structure:** Thirteen Parts plus eight Appendices (A–H) plus five Handouts (A–E) plus Glossary plus Recommended Reading. Part 1 establishes the three foundational principles (Just Culture, Systems Thinking, Learning); Part 2 distinguishes the four learning tools (AAR, RLS, FLA, Learning Review); Part 3 sets the Delegating Official's pre-team decisions; Parts 4–9 walk the FLA process from initiation through learning-product completion; Parts 10–11 add the Learning Review and Learning Review Board; Parts 12–13 collect appendices and handouts; the Glossary and Recommended Reading close.
**Citation style:** `(Part N, "Section name")` for body parts, `(Appendix X, "Section name")` for appendices, `(Handout X, "Section name")` for handouts, `(Glossary, "Term")` for glossary entries. The PDF was converted with pymupdf4llm 0.2.9; page anchors were not preserved by the conversion, so section names from the converted markdown are used in their place.
**Coverage:** all body content (Parts 1–13, Appendices A–H, Handouts A–E, Glossary, Recommended Reading) was read in full. Several illustrative inline graphics, photographs, and decorative section break elements were skipped because the converter does not extract image content as text; the substantive captions and prose around those images were read. Image classification was deferred (text-only ingest).

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

The guide takes the explicit position that how an agency responds to an unintended outcome — accident, near-miss, or surprising non-accident — determines whether the organisation actually learns from it [AR] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"). The traditional response — find who broke the rule, blame them, return the system to safety — is described as "not only ineffective for improving safety related to a specific accident, but also damaging to the overall learning culture" [V] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"). The guide's organising claim is that learning requires a coherent stance built from three intertwined foundational principles: *Just Culture* (the work environment of trust, fairness and dignity in which honest accounts can be given), *Systems Thinking* (examination of the policies, procedures, processes and culture that shaped the conditions around an event, rather than the individual at the sharp end), and *Learning* (the treatment of unintended outcomes as a "valued learning opportunity and as a means to reducing uncertainty of future outcomes") [AR] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"; Part 1, "Just Culture"; Part 1, "Learning").

A second load-bearing claim is that human performance variability is the rule, not the exception, and that adaptation and innovation are what produces successful outcomes most of the time. "It is impossible to predict all the potential situations that will arise in complex systems, and people are relied upon to make sense of these emerging conditions, learn in the moment, and innovate and adapt solutions to fit the situation" [V] (Part 1, "Part 1 – Foundational Principles"). "Employees make mistakes, but most of the time (almost continuously) they are actually *creating safety*" [V] (Part 1, "Part 1 – Foundational Principles"). The guide treats "human performance variability" as "not only normal, it's the rule" [V] (Part 1, "Learning").

A third load-bearing claim is that recommendations and causal statements impede learning in complex systems and should be avoided in the FLA — the *Learning Review* (a distinct, more expensive process) is the place where vetted recommendations may be produced if a Delegating Official is willing to own them [AR] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"; Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?"; Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors"). "FLAs must avoid causal statements" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). "Cause isn't something investigators 'find' or 'discover'; cause is always something we create by recreating the event showing how certain anomalies led to the accident" [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors"). The replacement concept is *networked causality* — "the combined total of those conditions that collectively influenced the event or outcome" [V] (Glossary, "Networked causality").

A fourth load-bearing claim is that "the story" — a factual, sensory, perspective-faithful narrative reconstruction of how the event made sense to the participants at the time — is the central learning artefact of the FLA. "Storytelling is widely recognized by educators as the most effective tool for experiential teaching and leading cultural change within an organization" [V] (Part 8, "Why FLA Storytelling?"). "Cognitive scientists have shown that humans make sense of, and remember, 'facts' by attaching them to narratives (i.e., stories) that give them context and a sensory or emotional association" [V] (Part 8, "Why FLA Storytelling?"). "In this way, storytelling moves Lessons Learned into the vicarious experience of Lessons Lived" [V] (Part 8, "Why FLA Storytelling?").

## Part I: The three foundational principles (Part 1)

### Systems Thinking — accidents as system-shaped, not person-caused

Systems thinking is presented as the alternative to the blame frame. "When an accident happens, system thinking enables us to examine how the organization's policies, procedures, processes and culture may have influenced the circumstances around the event" [V] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"). The diagnostic question is framed in *work-as-imagined vs work-as-done* terms: "How were we actually completing the work vs how managers were expecting the work to be completed?" [V] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"). "Systems thinking enables change in complex contexts by looking at the world in terms of wholes and relationships" [V] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking"). The Glossary entry confirms the framing: "a way of making sense of the complexity of the world by looking at it in terms of wholes and relationships rather than by splitting it down into its parts" [V] (Glossary, "Systems Thinking").

### Just Culture — forward-looking accountability over criminal-style culpability

Just Culture is defined as the intersection of trust, fairness, and dignity, with an open and safe reporting system in which "everyone can speak" [V] (Part 1, "Just Culture"). The guide draws an explicit contrast with criminal justice: "In the criminal justice arena accountability means punishments and correction. In our Just Culture, accountability means creating the space for each person involved to provide an honest account of what happened from his or her perspective" [V] (Part 1, "Just Culture"). The Glossary makes the etymology explicit: "Accountability: An account is something you tell and learn from" [V] (Glossary, "Accountability"). The guide also names the antonym slang: "Accountabilism: a slang term which refers to circumstances when an investigation or review effort claims the intent is to learn from participants sharing their 'accounts' but then punishes via judgmental language, requiring training or other organizational actions which punish" [V] (Glossary, "Accountabilism").

The just-culture framing is forward-looking: "This definition of just culture is focused on forward-looking accountability. It is concerned with sharing accounts of people involved in unintended outcomes to highlight the weaknesses and strengths in the system; to help us learn ways to improve ourselves. It is concerned with preventing the next accident, not focused on correcting history" [V] (Part 1, "Just Culture").

Restorative justice is named as a fourth component: "After a serious accident, employees are often emotionally harmed, and relationships can be strained or broken. With its focus on transparency and rejection of blame, the FLA process seeks to restore victims to wholeness" [V] (Part 1, "Just Culture"). Appendix A elaborates: "A mature just culture is characterized by openness and trust" [V] (Appendix A, "A Deeper Dive Into Just Culture"); "In a Just Culture, there is consensus among all employees that information (learning) is the lifeblood of safety" [V] (Appendix A, "A Deeper Dive Into Just Culture"). The Sidney Dekker / Hugh Breakey 2016 *Safety Science* paper on substantive, procedural, and restorative justice is cited as the academic source [BT] (Glossary, "Restorative justice", footnote 14).

### Learning — variability is the rule, not the exception

The Learning principle treats adaptation and innovation as continuous and normal rather than exceptional. "Errors, mistakes, and lapses are inherent in the human condition. So is taking shortcuts to save money, time, and effort as well as under and overestimating risk" [V] (Part 1, "Learning"). "Indeed, human performance variability is not only normal, it's the rule!" [V] (Part 1, "Learning"). The learning frame inverts the standard treatment of accidents: "If leaders recognize that this variability will always be a part of the overall organization, they can treat accidents and other unintended outcomes as precious opportunities to look deeply into the operation to better understand how employees perceive and manage risk" [V] (Part 1, "Learning").

Organisational learning is treated at multiple scales: grass-roots story-sharing (Mann Gulch is named via Maclean's *Young Men and Fire* as the canonical example) [AE] (Part 1, "What is organizational learning?"); leadership-level adaptation of equipment, policy, and training (the Ten Standard Firefighting Orders, the fire shelter, and the Incident Command System are named as products of this level) [AE] (Part 1, "What is organizational learning?"); and complex FLAs / Learning Reviews with explicit Lessons Learned Analyses (Part 1, "What is organizational learning?").

### Lineage — how the process got here

The historical section traces the lineage from criminal prosecution of firefighters after the Thirtymile and Cramer fatalities through the 2005 I-90 Tarkio Fire Entrapment learning-focused investigation, the 2006 "Peer Review" briefing paper, and the formal adoption of the FLA and Learning Review processes by the Forest Service in July 2013 [AP] (Part 1, "How we got here"). "In 2005, two members from the USFS Fire Operations Risk Management Council pushed the limits of policy by conducting a learning-focused serious accident investigation on the I-90 Tarkio Fire Entrapment" [V] (Part 1, "How we got here"). The Thirtymile, Cramer, and Esperanza cluster is presented as a real-world illustration of what happens without a Just Culture: "Our employees were criminally prosecuted in the aftermath of the Thirtymile and Cramer fires because at the time, extremely complex problems were perceived as simple problems with simplistic solutions. Then our employees refused to talk to the accident investigation team looking into the Esperanza Fire fatalities because they did not feel like they were going to be treated fairly" [V] (Appendix A, "A Deeper Dive Into Just Culture").

## Part II: The four tools and how to choose between them (Part 2)

The guide identifies four distinct tools for responding to outcomes, scaling from immediate-local to organisational-system level.

**AAR (After-Action Review).** "A powerful tool to capture immediate, local learning. AARs are predominantly closed, personal, and confidential, enabling participants to speak freely about mistakes. The learning that occurs from an AAR typically is at the individual level, local in nature, not shared beyond the work unit (crew or team) and does not affect the organization as a whole" [V] (Part 2, "The tools"). The AAR's product stays inside the work unit; the involved group "moves on, goes back to work, while learning from mistakes and building on successes" [V] (Part 2, "The tools").

**RLS (Rapid Lessons Sharing).** Expands on the AAR by producing "a simple learning product — focused on a near-miss or accident, or a new tool, technique, process or protocol — that can be shared with anyone who might benefit" [V] (Part 2, "The tools"). The Wildland Fire Lessons Learned Center hosts a submission tool that allows anonymous lesson posting [AP] (Part 2, "The tools").

**FLA (Facilitated Learning Analysis).** Has a signed delegation of authority "assuring participants that there shall be no punitive actions" [V] (Part 2, "The tools"). "An FLA is a scalable process used to make sense of an unintended outcome and develop lessons learned for the broader organization without recommendations" [V] (Part 2, "The tools"). Scales from a "basic FLA" (a couple of people, completed within a week) to a "complex FLA" (5 to 15 people including SMEs, working weeks or months) (Part 2, "The tools"). Output is a learning product — historically a written report, now expanding to multimedia.

**Learning Review.** "The key difference between the other tools and a LR is that the delegating authority for a LR has requested recommendations as an output of the review process" [V] (Part 2, "The tools"). The LR takes the FLA's product and adds community-of-practice focus groups, academic SMEs, and a Learning Review Board to vet and approve recommendations and produce a Safety Action Plan (Part 2, "The tools"; Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?"; Part 11, "Learning Review Board").

The guide explicitly distinguishes the FLA/LR family from the Serious Accident Investigation (SAI) process used by DOI agencies: "the SAI process relies on a root cause analysis, where the FLA and Learning Review processes focuses on conditions and context surrounding the event and relies on the concept of networked causality" [V] (Part 2, "The tools").

## Part III: Delegating Official decisions before the team forms (Part 3)

The Delegating Official's job, before a team is formed, is to choose a process and write its delegation of authority. The selection criterion the guide warns against is severity-of-outcome alone — "the natural tendency is to base the size and complexity of the review team upon the severity of the outcome. Often, the more 'serious the accident,' the more resources are put toward the review team. This tendency may pretermit stellar learning opportunities" [V] (Part 3, "Starting the process"). The illustrative case is a tree-cutting accident in which a firefighter was knocked unconscious by a glancing blow and released the same day; the guide argues this "non-significant" outcome would, with a two-inch difference in tree fall, have been a fatality, and that the choice to learn or not learn should not depend on a chance occurrence of two inches [AE] (Part 3, "Starting the process"; Part 3, "Questions to consider when deciding to mobilize a team").

The decision-support questions (Part 3, "Questions to consider when deciding to mobilize a team") include: how deep will you look? is there enough trust here to support such an analysis? are there conflicting stories surrounding the event? regardless of the outcome, do people close to the accident believe it could have been much more severe? what could be the cost of not choosing an FLA in terms of opportunities lost? are recommendations going to be an essential part of this report (necessitating a learning review)?

The guide makes a critical scope-extension explicit: "The AAR, RLS, FLA and LR processes were designed to be tools for accident investigation and learning from accident-like unintended outcomes. We should use the applicable process to review events that surprise us to make sense of the events and try to learn from them. However, these are also tools that have been used successfully to learn from events that were not accidents; including close call/near-miss events and events that weren't necessarily negative but had outcomes surprising to administrators" [V] (Part 3, "Learning beyond accidents"). The named examples include the Retardant Avoidance FLA, the Bear Meadows Stop Work FLA, the Burdette Fire Case Study, the Mud Lake Complex FLA, the Area Command COVID-19 FLA, and the "How Do We Honor the Fallen?" review [AE] (Part 3, "Learning beyond accidents").

The administrative-firewall commitment is formalised as: "It is critical to maintain a solid firewall between the FLA and any potential administrative actions that may be taken against the employee. Information is the lifeblood of safety. We must cherish it and protect it" [V] (Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions"). Administrative actions to be excluded include "any type of disciplinary actions and other adverse actions such as forced retraining, removal from an incident or reduction in planned overtime" [V] (Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions"). The Delegating Official cannot, however, offer immunity from external legal action.

The guide names *fundamental attribution error* as the cognitive trap of blaming employees: "Blaming employees can be a form of the Fundamental Attribution Error" [V] (Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions"). It cites Hollnagel's ETTO principle for the underlying tension: "Our employees always face conflicting goals and tensions between production and efficiency" [V] (Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions"), footnoted to *The ETTO Principle Efficiency-Thoroughness Trade-Off* [BT] (Part 3, footnote 2).

## Part IV: Forming the FLA team (Part 5)

After the Delegating Official's pre-decisions, Part 5 specifies team composition. Pre-team priorities are *take care of employees* (CISM, EAP, Family/Hospital Liaisons), *assign liaisons* (a trusted employee point of contact who can explain the process), and *manage concurrent investigations* (claims, prescribed fire reviews, law enforcement) (Part 5, "Priority Delegating Official Actions").

Note-taking guidance is grounded in memory research: "Research tells us that memories change; perspectives shift as time progresses. Research also shows us that memories are unreliable and its important to try to capture thoughts as quick as possible after an event" [V] (Part 5, "Should the affected people take Notes?"). Personal notes remain the employee's property; they will not be collected or read by anyone else.

Minimum team-member attributes (Part 5, "Forming the FLA team"):

- A basic understanding of Just Culture.
- A basic understanding of the FLA process (team leaders and facilitators should be formally trained or have experience under a trainer).
- A solid reputation for dealing with confidential matters.
- It is better not to have members from the local unit (or to be aware of the bias they bring if local members are used).
- Selection of Team Leader and Facilitator should consider the individual's ability to extricate themselves from the incident to avoid inputting personal bias into the process.

Roles listed (Part 5, "Potential FLA team members and roles"): FLA Process Coach, Learning Review Process Coach, Team Leader (the delegated official, accountable for quality and content), Lead Facilitator (the FLA process expert, analogous to "chief investigator" on a Serious Accident Investigation Team), Storyteller/Writer-Editor (a creative-nonfiction writer where one is available), Subject-Matter Experts/Peers (current peer to the affected role), Technical Specialists, NTDP equipment SMEs (mandatory consultation if PPE / burnover / shelter deployment is involved), the National Chainsaw Program Manager (mandatory for tree-falling/chainsaw accidents), the Missoula Fire Lab (for escape-route / safety-zone events), Human Performance experts via the Office of Innovation and Organizational Learning (IOL), FARO LIDAR operators (typically law-enforcement-community), Videographers and graphic designers, Union Representative (NFFE has been a strong supporter of Just Culture and the FLA process), Interagency Participation members, and Documentation Specialist (for complex FLAs with likely litigation).

The Common Operating Picture between FLA team and host unit is established through: clear mutual objectives (in-brief covering schedule, scope, expected outputs); understanding the fundamentals (purpose of an FLA vs other investigation types; the no-punitive-administrative-action assurance); trust (FLA team members must never betray confidentiality, with the only exception being judicial order outside agency control); and cooperation with other investigations (OSHA, OIG investigations proceed alongside the FLA but are kept procedurally separate, with material evidence shared but interview transcripts and team deliberations kept confidential) (Part 5, "A Common Operating Picture" through "Cooperation with Other Investigations").

### Recommendations vs lessons learned — the central design choice

The "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned" section is one of the guide's most argument-dense (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). "The old investigation paradigm has been that we prevent accidents by discovering their root cause, and then fixing the cause to prevent a repeat of the accident. This perspective is problematic in complex systems and highly subjective because they are always outcome dependent" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). "FLAs must avoid causal statements" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned").

The argument against recommendations as the default output is that "Managers used to the 'causal' model may jump straight to the 'Recommendations' section of the learning product, develop an action plan, and believe they've solved the problem. Readers may look to recommendations for security, 'If I'm following these recommendations, then I must be safe.' In both cases, our cultural baggage around recommendations may make learning harder because there is no zero-risk option to complete work" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned").

"In many circumstances, recommendations actually interfere with learning. Often recommendations, when implemented, add complexity to the workplace, which has the paradoxical effect of increasing risk" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). The guide's preferred alternative is to "consider ways to make the FLA learning product an effective and compelling learning tool" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"), with Hollnagel's ETTO principle cited as the underlying frame (Part 5, footnote 4).

The position is not absolute. "If the Delegating Official insists on having recommendations, the Learning Review process must be initiated so the recommendations can be vetted with the communities of practice and a Safety Action Plan can be developed that outlines who is responsible to carry them out and by what date" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). "We argue against providing recommendations on the basis of a single data point" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned").

## Part V: Engaging the process — site visits, interviews, group dialogue (Part 6)

The heart of the FLA process is described as "dialogue with the participants" [V] (Part 6, "Site visits, Interviews and Group Dialogue sessions"). Three engagement modalities — site visits, individual interviews, and the facilitated group dialogue — are treated as the means through which the foundational principles are put into practice.

### Setting the stage and giving participants control

"Throughout the FLA process, team members should be prepared to explain the history of FLA and its major underpinnings, such as the degree of confidentiality, Just Culture and The Gap" [V] (Part 6, "Setting the stage"). The "Gap" is defined in the Glossary as "the difference between what administrators think is going on and what is really going on in the field" [V] (Glossary, "GAP").

"For both individual and group dialogue sessions, the team should attempt to provide as comfortable environment as possible. Perhaps the best way to accomplish this is to give as much control as possible to the participants" [V] (Part 6, "Site visits, Interviews and Group Dialogue sessions"). The practical examples — engine barn, saw shop, tac shed, picnic tables — are framed as ways to keep participants from "feeling like they are being interrogated" [V] (Part 6, "Site visits, Interviews and Group Dialogue sessions").

### Interviews

"The intent of an interview is to capture the person's story" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews"). For complex FLAs, extensive interviewing "should not be hurried to meet an arbitrary date" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews"). Key participants should be interviewed by a team of two — one taking notes, the other engaging — with the caveat that two interviewers can be intimidating and should be used "with appropriate caution and understanding" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews").

A specific *deflection-question* protocol is given for handling emotional recall: "if the person takes the interview to a difficult point of recall don't leave them there mentally. Be prepared with a few deflection questions to mentally 'walk the person away' from these vivid memories they are sharing with you" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews"). The worked examples are unusually concrete — the interviewer redirects from a witness's account of an engine going over a road edge to "take me back to that morning when you guys were at the engine bay, what time did your duty day start? What's a normal day look like for you guys?" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews"). The justification: "Deflection questions use inquiry to ask the witness about something that went right or something they know very well and are comfortable talking about" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews").

The guide also instructs interviewers to "battle bias (see Handout C FLA 'Watch Outs')" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews") and to "focus on their objective to be able to describe how the people involved developed their understanding of the situation and made sense of their choices at the time and in context" [V] (Part 6, "Interviews"). Daily team debriefs are mandated.

### Principles and Agreements for the Group Dialogue

The ten "Principles and Agreements for All Involved in the Facilitation" (Part 6, "Principles and Agreements for All Involved in the Facilitation") are the procedural commitment for the group dialogue session, given verbatim where load-bearing:

> "1. We have clear agreement with the Delegating Official that no administrative actions, (that is, disciplinary actions such as letters of caution, stand-downs, and forced re-trainings) may result from anything learned through the FLA process. If there is any question about this, stop and clear up confusion.
> 2. Respectful discussion is the rule; it can be emotional but it must remain respectful.
> 3. Learning for future events is more important than assessing past blame.
> 4. Be willing to be vulnerable. This discussion is not to find who 'caused' the accident. We are here only to share what each individual has learned from the incident and then see if we can turn that into collective learning.
> 5. The FLA team is not here to 'fix' a problem. This is only about taking advantage of an opportunity to learn. If anyone would rather use this session to prove someone else was wrong, they are invited to leave.
> 6. We all make mistakes—it's inevitable; it's the human condition. It's okay to openly discuss these occurrences.
> 7. Almost all human actions and decisions are intuitive responses to circumstances largely based on past experiences. It is extremely rare that our people are actually careless, meaning that they didn't care if the outcome was or was not as intended.
> 8. Accidents are almost always the result of rare combinations of normal performance variability and chance combinations of unlikely events.
> 9. Safety is never an absolute.
> 10. Within this dialogue, safety should be thought of, and referred to, as the reasonableness of risk. It is a feeling about which two experienced, competent professionals can disagree—and both be right!" [V] (Part 6, "Principles and Agreements for All Involved in the Facilitation").

### Facilitation moves

Specific facilitator moves recommended (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session"):

- **Location, location, location.** "It is basically the 'stage' for the FLA performance. The best location is almost always the field where the incident occurred" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session"). If field is not available, "don't just accept the 'available conference room.'"
- **You are not your point of view.** Open with a story where a smart person was absolutely convinced things were one way and turned out wrong; "the goal is to get people to feel that they don't have to defend their perspective" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session"). "Get agreement with your audience upfront that nobody's credibility is on the line" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").
- **Invite uncertainty.** "Dare people to suspend any certainty they have in what happened and challenge them to anticipate (and even imagine) that over the next hour or two, they will be surprised by what they didn't know" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").
- **Exploit points of departure.** "Points of departure refers to the moments of inconsistency that occur in our accounts of the event that reflect the different perspectives that members hold. The idea is not to validate one story over the other but to reveal when these discrepancies occur so that all involved have a chance to see from another's perspective" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").
- **Encourage sensemaking, not adjudication.** "The goal of the group dialogue session is to encourage sensemaking through dialogue to achieve a better understanding of the multiple perspectives of the participants" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").
- **Listen for the silent voice.** "Many people, even some extroverts, do not feel comfortable speaking up in a group of peers. Some great thinkers don't feel confident in their ability to think on their feet, developing coherent arguments while talking at the same time. The facilitator needs be attentive these quiet participants and work them into the dialogue. Don't allow a self-directed dialogue to continue very long unless everyone is participating" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").

The agenda for the dialogue session is deliberately loose: "Only a general agenda is necessary. It should not constrain the flow of the discussion. Experienced facilitators have learned to ensure there is more than enough time available; dialogues often go for several hours or longer" [V] (Part 6, "Agenda for the Dialogue Session"). Sand tables, Google Earth, and ArcMap are named as physical/digital aids that help reconstruct what people perceived at the time (Part 6, "Sand Tables, Google Earth, and ArcMap").

## Part VI: The complex FLA — Lessons Learned Analysis and the Five Hows (Part 7)

The Lessons Learned Analysis is the dialogue tool that distinguishes a complex FLA from a basic one. The opening epigram is a Dekker & Pruchnicki quote: "Actions that are interpreted as 'bad decisions' after an adverse event are, at the same time actions that seem reasonable – or people would not have taken them" [V] (Part 7, "The Lessons Learned Analysis", quoting Dekker & Pruchnicki 2013). The FLA team's stance follows: it "does not see wrongdoing, but rather tries to understand how people can see their actions as being right" [V] (Part 7, "The Lessons Learned Analysis", quoting Dekker & Pruchnicki).

"A Lesson Learned Analysis is a confidential team dialogue... The task is for the team to achieve the same level situational awareness, belief, and expectation that was held by those involved in the accident — which they had before the accident. The end-state is the insight generated from the dialogue itself. Consensus is not the goal. Tension between individual team member's perspectives is valuable" [V] (Part 7, "The Lessons Learned Analysis").

The *Five Hows* is the framing tool for the Lessons Learned Analysis. The guide presents the Five Hows as questions to be applied to each key action or decision in the event, with the explicit instruction that "You may find some of these 'hows' do not apply and some will be redundant. Use those that are helpful and don't waste time trying to cook-book the process" [V] (Part 7, "The Five Hows – A Sensemaking Tool"). The intent is to "explore how and why the decisions and actions that seemed wrong in hindsight were actually valid or at least legitimate in context at the time" [V] (Part 7, "The Five Hows – A Sensemaking Tool"). Source note: the specific list of five "Hows" is presented in the source as a graphic that did not extract to text in the PDF conversion; the prose explanation above is what the source text says about the tool.

The Lessons Learned Analysis procedure (Part 7, "A Process for conducting a Lessons Learned Analysis") is given as seven steps:

1. Gather the FLA team together in a secure, private meeting room.
2. Post the quotes and key bits of information from the interviews on flip chart paper and sticky notes, highlighting what participants learned for themselves and what they want management or fellow employees to learn.
3. Discuss the useful and interesting quotes — surprising, troubling, or hard-to-empathise-with statements or actions.
4. Identify interesting, odd, or disturbing lessons learned. For each, discuss the beliefs, perceptions, expectations, and paradigms held before the experience that in hindsight turned out wrong, inaccurate, misleading, or deceptive.
5. Identify and discuss the "key decisions" or "key actions" that flowed from those beliefs, perceptions, expectations, and paradigms.
6. Agree as a team on the most important key decisions or actions; for each, deliberate on the Five Hows in dialogue.
7. From this dialogue, write up the Lesson Learned Analysis explaining the sensemaking that occurred before and during the event and why, in context, the actions and decisions made sense.

"A successful Lessons Learned Analysis results in the team being less judgmental of the participants actions" [V] (Part 7, "The Lessons Learned Analysis"). "Most often, the Lessons Learned Analysis will reveal that multiple improbable events were necessary for the accident to occur" [V] (Part 7, "The Lessons Learned Analysis").

## Part VII: Storytelling — the FLA's primary learning artefact (Part 8)

"In an FLA, 'the story' should not be confused with fiction or an enhancement of facts. An FLA's story is a factual description of what occurred" [V] (Part 8, "Why FLA Storytelling?"). The cognitive justification: "Cognitive scientists have shown that humans make sense of, and remember, 'facts' by attaching them to narratives (i.e., stories) that give them context and a sensory or emotional association. If given a listing of findings with no story as context, our minds are prone to give these findings relevance by either creating a story, or by associating them with a story that is already within our memory. In other words, the lack of the story behind the 'facts' will actually lead to a distortion of the facts within our minds" [V] (Part 8, "Why FLA Storytelling?").

The guide emphasises perspective-respecting storytelling: "It is inevitable in any complex event that the people involved in the accident will have different perspectives and memories of what happened, and how, and why. Often the facilitated dialogue session will reconcile disparate stories. Occasionally however there will remain very different accounts of what happened. Don't see this as somebody being right and somebody being wrong" [V] (Part 8, "Different Perspectives"). The footnoted Dekker, Cilliers, Hofmeyr 2011 paper on *The Complexity of Failure* is cited verbatim: "respect otherness, difference in accounts about what happened as a value in itself. Diversity of narrative can be seen as an enormous source of resilience in complex system not as weakness. The more angles the more there can be to learn" [V] (Part 8, "Different Perspectives", quoting Dekker, Cilliers, Hofmeyr, *Safety Science* 49 (2011), p. 944).

The named storytelling craft elements (Part 8, "Storytelling Basics: tips for creative nonfiction storytellers") draw on Lisa Cron's *Wired for Story* [BT] (Handout E, "Tips for Facilitated Learning Analysis Storytelling", footnote 13):

- **Set up main character(s) without identifying information.** Tell us who these people are: their experiences, backgrounds, limited personal family backgrounds, physical traits — enough for readers to relate.
- **Set up the place.** "The fact that it was hot or cold, steep or flat, dusty or damp, may have no relevance to the actual event but these sensory details are necessary if we are to vicariously join the characters in their experience" [V] (Part 8, "Storytelling Basics").
- **Connect main characters with the place** — backstory, environment, how the characters arrived at the moment.
- **Share the character's internal voice** — what they want to achieve and why; differences between personal goals and public/outer goals; differences between what they want to do and what they are expected to do.
- **Challenges and obstacles or conflict** — "There are trade-offs between efficiency and thoroughness. There are always goal conflicts. Tell the reader what these are for the person" [V] (Part 8, "Storytelling Basics").
- **Sensemaking and decision making.** "These decisions must have seemed reasonable at the time given the conditions the character understood at the time. This does not mean that the storyteller is defending the decisions; rather, they are only relating how decisions made sense at the time" [V] (Part 8, "Storytelling Basics").

The *Readback* process (Part 8, "Validating Stories through the Readback process") gives all key participants the chance to hear the finalised learning product story read aloud by the FLA Team and to correct or clarify important details. "Remember this is their story, not yours" [V] (Part 8, "Validating Stories through the Readback process"). Readbacks occur in two phases — first to those directly involved, then to other participants, supervisors, and administrators. Hard copies are collected afterward to prevent contradictory drafts from circulating.

## Part VIII: Final FLA considerations and termination (Part 9)

The "Avoid Counterfactual Arguments" instruction is named explicitly: "FLA teams must guard against making counterfactual arguments such as: 'If this person had done X, then the outcome would have been Y and the accident would not have occurred.' The FLA is only useful when it learns why people did what they actually did (why it made sense to them at the time), rather than why they did not do something that — in hindsight — others might think they should have" [V] (Part 9, "Avoid Counterfactual Arguments").

The *Display Misalignments* step is the work-as-imagined / work-as-done diagnostic explicitly named: "Once the FLA team understands how the accident participants made sense of their environment, the team should contrast this understanding with how administrators thought employees would (or should) make sense of the environment. Illuminating the gap between work as imagined by administrators and work as actually accomplished will illuminate substantial and critical organizational vulnerabilities" [V] (Part 9, "Display Misalignments Between Different Aspects or Roles within the Organization").

### Termination criterion: reckless and willful disregard

"During the course of the FLA process, though highly unlikely, it could be discovered that an agency employee acted with a reckless and willful disregard for human safety or committed a serious criminal act" [V] (Part 9, "Serious Crimes or Reckless and Willful Disregard for Human Safety"). In such cases, "the event may no longer be appropriate for a 'safety investigation' (FLA). Why? Because the event is no longer considered an 'accident'" [V] (Part 9, "Serious Crimes or Reckless and Willful Disregard for Human Safety"). The FLA team leader writes a memo to the Delegating Official terminating the FLA and recommending an administrative or law enforcement investigation; physical evidence is released to the Delegating Official; interview notes are sealed by the lead facilitator; "FLA team members must not discuss anything they learned during the FLA process with anyone" [V] (Part 9, "Serious Crimes or Reckless and Willful Disregard for Human Safety") apart from cooperation with external authorities outside agency control.

The reckless-and-willful threshold is defined in Appendix C: "A reckless and willful disregard for human safety is conduct that is intentional, unjustifiable, and occurred with the foreknowledge that the conduct was likely to result in serious harm, death, or injury to a human" [V] (Appendix C, "Is an FLA the Right Tool?"). "FLAs are for the vast majority of events in which good people, doing what they thought made sense at the time, ended up in an unexpected situation" [V] (Appendix C, "Is an FLA the Right Tool?").

## Part IX: The Learning Review (Parts 10–11)

The Learning Review extends the FLA when the Delegating Official wants vetted recommendations and is willing to own them. The conceptual frame is analysis-vs-synthesis: "Analysis is the go-to method for gaining understanding in the western world. It involves breaking things down into components and then trying to figure out which component is broken. This has proven to be a very useful method, especially with respect to machines... The machine metaphor is not as widely applicable as our culture seems to give it credit for" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?"). "Life is an emergent property that cannot be fully examined through analysis" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?"). "Synthesis is the process of trying to understand how emergent properties come to be. It is a study of how things are put together (rather than taken apart)" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?"). The LR "incorporates both analysis and synthesis through sensemaking" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?").

The LR's diagnostic questions: "Are our policies keeping up with a changing system of work?" "Is our organization sending mixed messages to field-going personnel?" "Do our employees face goal conflicts of which management is [unaware]?" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?").

For mechanical, ergonomic, and structural failures, the LR explicitly admits engineering-based analysis: "'analysis' is applicable only to objective information and is conducted to assess mechanical, ergonomic, and structural failures. It follows an engineering-based model, which is dedicated to problem-solving using quantitative data" [V] (Part 10, "How the learning review process examines mechanical, ergonomic and structural failures"). The output is often a *barrier* (tangible — physical or mechanical — or intangible — rules, regulations, policies, procedures). "Barriers have been shown to be effective in simple and complicated systems. Therefore, barriers may be appropriate in cases where analysis can successfully provide answers to problems. Complex systems are non-linear by definition, and barriers have proven to be less effective in social (complex) situations" [V] (Part 10, "How the learning review process examines mechanical, ergonomic and structural failures"). Sidney Dekker's *The Field Guide to Understanding Human Error* (Ashgate 2014) is cited verbatim for the sensemaking frame: "in complex situations, people must act based on uncertain, incomplete, and sometimes even contradictory information" [V] (Part 10, "The learning review process", footnote 8).

The LR uses *focus groups* drawn from the community of practice — line officers, engine crewmembers, hotshots, helitack, wildland fire modules, sawyers, trail crew, timber markers, etc. — to vet recommendations and "concentrate on understanding the applicability of conditions to the actual (real) world of how work is accomplished" [V] (Part 10, "2. Preparing to Hold the First Focus Group"). External academic SMEs may also participate. The output is a Network of Influences map plus a complex narrative plus a sensemaking document plus proposed recommendations, brought before a Learning Review Board chaired by the Delegating Official (Part 10, "An overview of the process"; Part 11, "Learning Review Board").

Once the LRB approves the recommendations, they become a *Safety Action Plan*: "The SAP will designate who is responsible for completing the recommendation(s). Those responsible for the completion of an action item listed on the SAP should be consulted prior to committing any resource to an action item or recommendation. This is designed to avoid unnecessary requests for extensions" [V] (Part 11, "Recommendations").

Two learning products are produced (Part 11, "Learning Review Products"):

- **Organizational Learning Product** — "directed to answer the specific needs of the organization, in terms of understanding the organizational, structural, systemic, and cultural contributors to the event." Designed for policy-makers and higher levels. "Products should help these individuals to understand the gap between 'work as imagined' and 'work as performed'" [V] (Part 11, "Organizational Learning Product").
- **Field Learning Product** — designed for district or field-office employees. "Lessons should be emphasized emotively through dialogue, experientially through simulation, or by engaging the readers in personal reflection regarding their own experiences" [V] (Part 11, "Field Learning Product"). "The team should not feel limited to conventional written reports; they should consider creative tools such as high-tech graphics and multimedia presentations" [V] (Part 11, "Field Learning Product").

## Part X: Bias and watch-outs (Handout C, Handout D, Appendix B)

Handout C names four biases plus one habit of thought as the FLA team's recurring watch-outs (Handout C, "FLA 'Watch outs'"). The body text identifies hindsight bias explicitly: "Human memory connects images and facts to build a coherent mental story that makes sense in the light of the now known outcome. This is not a conscious process. 'Sensemaking' (see definition in glossary) does not stop after the accident. It is common to interview a firefighter involved in an accident and find their language packed with things they wished they had done differently. Then, in the subsequent weeks, this person's language changes to the certainty of what other people should have done" [V] (Handout C, "FLA 'Watch outs'"). The recommended counter-move is to interview as soon as possible and "help keep them focused on telling the story solely from their perspective" [V] (Handout C, "FLA 'Watch outs'"). Note: the specific list of "four biases plus a way of thinking" is presented in the source as a graphic block that did not extract to text; the surrounding prose names hindsight bias and the fundamental attribution error (the latter named explicitly in Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions").

The Roese & Vohs 2012 *Perspectives on Psychological Science* paper on hindsight bias is named in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading, "Accident, Safety and Human Performance").

Handout D presents two dialogue styles for facilitated group dialogue: AAR-type questions (the standard AAR's five questions) and *Organizing for High Reliability*-type questions, drawn from Weick and Sutcliffe's High Reliability Organisations work [BT] (Handout D, "Two Different Styles for Dialogue Focused Questions"). The HRO questions cluster around the five HRO hallmarks: preoccupation with failure, reluctance to oversimplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, deference to expertise [V] (Glossary, "High Reliability Organization"). Example HRO-style questions named in the source (Handout D, "Two Different Styles for Dialogue Focused Questions"):

- "What were we sure that we do not want to misestimate? How well did we do? Did we do a pre-mortem? What were our trigger points and how effective were they?"
- "Where was our attention? How did we identify what was going on?"
- "What cues were we paying attention to in the environment? Where did we contain small errors before they had big consequences? What surprised us?"
- "Who did we listen to? Who did we not listen to?"
- "Was this a 'brutal audit'? What did we learn about our system, including brittle points where small errors had big consequences? Where do we need to build in more redundancy or more slack?"

Appendix B's argument against causal factors is one of the guide's strongest position statements:

> "There is a deliberate effort in the FLA process to avoid labeling human errors, omissions or other actions (or non-actions) as 'causal.' Labeling these findings as 'cause' impedes our ability to explain or understand what was experienced in context before the accident. Constructing causal statements in complex systems inevitably degrades our ability to understand the complex nature of accidents, the role of chance, and nature of human performance functioning in dynamic environments." [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors").

> "Causal statements inevitably lack context because, for one, context is too complex; and two, because once context is explained you'll find cause becomes a conclusion that no longer follows from the premise (a non sequitur). Cause isn't something investigators 'find' or 'discover'; cause is always something we create by recreating the event showing how certain anomalies led to the accident." [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors").

> "Practitioners at the 'sharp end' (those workers who directly confront the risks of the workplace) are inheritors of policies, training programs, tools, culture, incentives, etc., that are the responsibility of those managers and administrators at the 'blunt-end.'" [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors").

The Hollnagel 2008 *Resilience Engineering Perspectives* reading is cited verbatim: "Accidents are more accurately understood as unexpected combinations of normal performance variability (both human and system performance variability)" [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors", attributing Hollnagel 2008, p. 75).

## Part XI: Risk-as-feeling and the safety-vs-production tradeoff (Handout A)

Handout A condenses the guide's position on risk and safety into bullets (Handout A, "Understanding the Work Under a Just Culture"):

- "Risk is inherent in everything we do. Short of never doing anything, there is no way to avoid all risk or ever to be 100% safe" [V] (Handout A).
- "How employees (at any level) perceive, anticipate, interpret, and react to risk is systematically connected to conditions associated with the design, systems, features, and culture of the workplace" [V] (Handout A).
- Risk-as-construct, attributed to Paul Slovic (quoted via Kahneman's *Thinking Fast and Slow*, 2011, p. 141): "Risk does not exist 'out there,' independent of our minds and culture, waiting to be measured. Human beings have invented the concept of 'risk' to help them understand and cope with the dangers and the uncertainties of life. Although these dangers are real, there is no such thing as a 'real risk' or 'objective risk'" [V] (Handout A, "Understanding the Work Under a Just Culture", quoting Slovic via Kahneman).
- "The best definition of 'safety' is: the reasonableness of risk. It is a feeling. It is not an absolute. It is personal and contextual and will vary between people even within identical situations" [V] (Handout A).
- "While safety is an essential business practice, our agency does not exist to be safe or to protect our employees. We exist to accomplish a taxpayer-funded mission as efficiently as possible — knowing that many activities we choose to perform are inherently hazardous" [V] (Handout A).
- "Essentially every risk mitigation (every safety precaution) carries some level of 'cost' to production or compromise to efficiency... Employees at all levels (administrators, safety advisors, system designers, and front-line employees) are continuously — and often subconsciously — estimating, balancing, optimizing, managing, and accepting these subtle and nuanced tradeoffs between safety and production" [V] (Handout A).
- "Our natural intuition (our common sense) is to let outcomes draw the line between success and failure and to base safety programs on outcomes. This is shortsighted and eventually dangerous... Risk Management is wholly concerned with managing risks, not outcomes. Risk management is counterintuitive" [V] (Handout A).

## Part XII: Lessons and Advice for FLA Teams (Appendix E)

Practitioner advice consolidated across years of FLA implementations (Appendix E, "Lessons and Advice for fla teams"):

- Don't forget the overall health and wellbeing of yourself and your team — pulse check. Make arrangements for work and personal life before taking an assignment.
- Take the time to build the Team and Align Team Values. "Make sure everyone reads the FLA guide" [V] (Appendix E). Plan for team-cohesion work to take at least a couple of hours. Use Handout A to drive a respectful dialogue point-by-point.
- Everyone on the team should be able to explain the FLA process in a succinct manner. Each member should think about their individual *why* for being on the team.
- Choose the Local Liaison Wisely and Put the Liaison to Work Quickly. Multiple liaisons may be needed (logistics, spokesperson, hospital, CISM, family, regional/state office, cooperator, agency rep, IMT).
- Utilise a process coach to provide an impartial view or advice for the team.
- Lay Out the Road Map and Adjust as Needed. "Don't just tell team members to 'trust the process'. People want know where they are and what is going to happen next and know they are making progress" [V] (Appendix E).
- Debrief with the Team Every Evening. The lead facilitator schedules end-of-day sharing.
- Stay Together. "Keep the team physically together until you have a solid draft. Letting people go home and trying to complete the learning product by emails and conference calls will inevitably add problematic delays" [V] (Appendix E).
- Don't Skimp on Peer Participation (the guide flags this as CRITICAL). "Ensure that peers are part of the team. The importance of having people on the team that are 'peers' to the people involved in the unintended outcome cannot be overstated" [V] (Appendix E).

## Key concepts and definitions (from Glossary)

| Term | Source definition |
|---|---|
| Accountability | "An account is something you tell and learn from. Accountability is taking that story and looking towards the future..." [V] (Glossary, "Accountability"). |
| Accountabilism | Claiming to learn from accounts but then punishing via judgmental language or training-as-punishment [V] (Glossary, "Accountabilism"). |
| Conditions | "Factors which affected pertinent decisions and actions that took place during the event like environmental conditions... as well as social, cultural, and political influences" [V] (Glossary, "Conditions"). |
| Culpability | "Responsibility for wrongdoing or failure; moral/legal/criminal culpability" — what people often mean when they say *accountability* [V] (Glossary, "Culpability"). |
| Dialogue | "A conversation with a center, not sides. The intention of dialogue is to reach a new understanding. In dialogue, one seeks to uncover a base of shared meaning..." [V] (Glossary, "Dialogue", quoting Isaacs 1999). |
| ETTO (Efficiency-Thoroughness Trade-Off) | "If demands to productivity or performance are high, thoroughness is reduced until the productivity goals are met. If demands to safety are high, efficiency is reduced until the safety goals are met" [V] (Glossary, "Efficiency-Thoroughness Trade-Off", quoting Hollnagel). |
| GAP | "The difference between what administrators think is going on and what is really going on in the field" [V] (Glossary, "GAP"). |
| High Reliability Organization | "An infrastructure for organizations utilizing five principles with the intent of minimizing catastrophic events: preoccupation with failure, reluctance to oversimplify, sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience and deference to expertise" [V] (Glossary, "High Reliability Organization", citing Weick & Sutcliffe 2007). |
| Networked causality | "The combined total of those conditions that collectively influenced the event or outcome" [V] (Glossary, "Networked causality"). |
| Recognition primed decision making | "A model people can use experience to generate a plausible option as the first one they consider" [V] (Glossary, "Recognition primed decision making", citing Gary Klein). |
| Restorative justice | "An element of just culture which aims to restore the status of the individual involved and heal relationships and injuries of victims and the wider community in the wake of an ethical breach" [V] (Glossary, "Restorative justice", citing Dekker). |
| Retributive justice | "A post event/accident approach which focuses on determining the proper treatment of those who have breached certain standards of conduct. It is focused on the errors or violations of individuals" [V] (Glossary, "Retributive justice", citing Dekker). |
| Safety-vs-production | "The dynamic tension between an organization's desire to accomplish work (production) and its desire to keep its people free from harm or injury" [V] (Glossary, "Safety-vs-production"). |
| Sensemaking | "The process of creating situational awareness and understanding situations of high complexity or uncertainty to make decisions" [V] (Glossary, "Sensemaking"). |
| Sharp end / blunt end | Sharp end = workers directly confronting risk; blunt end = supervisors and administrators with strategic risk management and time-rich decision environments [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors"). |
| Storytelling | "The process in which we seek to take an event and share the knowledge and wisdom the participants of the unintended outcome experienced. An FLA's story is a factual description of what occurred told in a manner that seeks to share sensory detail..." [V] (Glossary, "Storytelling"). |

## Connections the author makes in the text

- **Sidney Dekker** — approving; cited verbatim multiple times. *Just Culture: Balancing Safety and Accountability* (2007) is the foundational read in Recommended Reading; *The Field Guide to Understanding Human Error* (2014) is cited verbatim in the Learning Review section (Part 10, "The learning review process", footnote 8); Dekker & Pruchnicki 2013 *Drifting into Failure* is the epigram for Part 7's Lessons Learned Analysis; Dekker, Cilliers, Hofmeyr 2011 *Safety Science* on the complexity of failure is the epigram for Part 8's perspective-respecting storytelling; Dekker & Breakey 2016 *Safety Science* on substantive/procedural/restorative justice is the citation for the Glossary's restorative-justice definition [BT] (multiple).
- **Erik Hollnagel** — approving; the ETTO principle is the framing for the safety-vs-production tradeoff (Part 3 footnote 2; Part 5; Handout A; Glossary, "Efficiency-Thoroughness Trade-Off"). Hollnagel 2008 *Resilience Engineering Perspectives* Vol 1 is cited for the "unexpected combinations of normal performance variability" framing in Appendix B [BT] (Appendix B, footnote 11).
- **Karl Weick and Kathleen Sutcliffe** — approving; *Managing the Unexpected* (2007) and the five HRO principles are cited in the Glossary's HRO entry and used to structure HRO-type dialogue questions in Handout D [BT] (Glossary; Handout D).
- **Gary Klein** — approving; Recognition Primed Decision Making is named in the Glossary [BT] (Glossary, "Recognition primed decision making").
- **Daniel Kahneman** — approving; *Thinking Fast and Slow* (2011) is in Recommended Reading and is the source of the Paul Slovic risk-as-construct quotation in Handout A [BT] (Handout A, footnote 12; Recommended Reading).
- **William Isaacs** — approving; *Dialogue and the Art of Thinking Together* (1999) is the source of the Glossary's "Dialogue" definition [BT] (Glossary, "Dialogue").
- **Simon Sinek** — approving but lightly; the Golden Circle is invoked as a framing device for the "why" of Just Culture in Appendix A [BT] (Appendix A, footnote 10).
- **Todd Conklin** — approving; *Pre-Accident Investigations* (2012) is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **James Reason** — approving; *Managing the Risks of Organizational Accidents* (1997) and *The Human Contribution* (2008) are in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **Carol Tavris and Elliot Aronson** — approving; *Mistakes Were Made (but not by me)* (2007) is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **Norman Maclean** — approving; *Young Men and Fire* is named as the canonical narrative-mediated learning artefact for Mann Gulch [BT] (Part 1, "What is organizational learning?").
- **Lisa Cron** — approving; *Wired for Story* (2012) is the source for the storytelling craft elements in Handout E [BT] (Handout E, footnote 13).
- **Jonathan Gottschall** — approving; *The Storytelling Animal* (2012) is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **Peter Senge** — approving; *The Fifth Discipline* is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **Edgar and Peter Schein** — approving; *Humble Inquiry* (2nd ed.) is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **David Marx** — approving; *Patient Safety and the "Just Culture"* (2001) is in Recommended Reading [BT] (Recommended Reading).
- **Paul Chamberlain** — named as the originator of the FLA: "Paul Chamberlin stated the intent of a FLA was to 'improve performance by capitalizing on the shared experiences of participants'" [V] (Part 9, "FLA Learning Products"). (Note: the source spells the name "Chamberlin" in this passage and "Chamberlain" in the Preface; the Preface spelling is used here.)

## Positions the source explicitly frames against

- **The blame-and-rule-tightening response to accidents.** "Following this logic, all that's needed are better rules or better compliance. And that's the end of story — until the next accident. History has shown us that this approach is not only ineffective for improving safety related to a specific accident, but also damaging to the overall learning culture" [V] (Part 1, "Systems Thinking").
- **The criminal-justice meaning of accountability.** "In the criminal justice arena accountability means punishments and correction. In our Just Culture, accountability means creating the space for each person involved to provide an honest account..." [V] (Part 1, "Just Culture").
- **Root-cause analysis in complex systems.** The Glossary defines RCA at the simple/complicated end of the spectrum and points to Appendix B for the deep-dive critique [V] (Glossary, "Root Cause or Root Cause Analysis"; Appendix B). The SAI process is explicitly named as the root-cause-based alternative the FLA/LR family departs from (Part 2, "The tools").
- **Causal statements in accident reports.** "FLAs must avoid causal statements" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned"). "Cause isn't something investigators 'find' or 'discover'; cause is always something we create..." [V] (Appendix B, "A Deeper Dive into Causal Factors").
- **Recommendations as the default output.** "In many circumstances, recommendations actually interfere with learning. Often recommendations, when implemented, add complexity to the workplace, which has the paradoxical effect of increasing risk" [V] (Part 5, "Recommendations vs Lessons Learned").
- **Counterfactual reasoning.** "FLA teams must guard against making counterfactual arguments" [V] (Part 9, "Avoid Counterfactual Arguments").
- **Severity-of-outcome as the criterion for review-team size.** "Often, the more 'serious the accident,' the more resources are put toward the review team. This tendency may pretermit stellar learning opportunities" [V] (Part 3, "Starting the process").
- **The machine metaphor for understanding social systems.** "The machine metaphor is not as widely applicable as our culture seems to give it credit for" [V] (Part 10, "What is a Learning Review?").
- **The Fundamental Attribution Error in incident analysis.** "Blaming employees can be a form of the Fundamental Attribution Error" [V] (Part 3, "Administrative assurance of no punitive actions").
- **The "we just need more training" reflex.** "When 'we just need more training' comes up… Try to dig deeper. Sometimes, that is the answer, but sometimes it's not. If you don't have on your PPE because you can't do your job properly and wear it, it's a bigger issue than training" [V] (Part 6, "Suggestions to FLA Facilitator for the Group Dialogue Session").

## Citation and source-integrity notes

The PDF was converted with pymupdf4llm 0.2.9 in a single pass; the source is born-digital from MS Word and the conversion was clean. Page anchors were not preserved by the conversion, so citations use `(Part N, "Section name")`, `(Appendix X, "Section name")`, `(Handout X, "Section name")`, and `(Glossary, "Term")` styles per the source's own structural divisions.

Two source elements did not extract as text and are flagged here for traceability: (1) the *Five Hows* list in Part 7 appears in the source as a graphic and is referenced in the deep ref only through the surrounding prose explanation; (2) the *FLA "Watch Outs"* list in Handout C appears as a graphic and is referenced only through the surrounding prose, which explicitly names hindsight bias as one of the watch-outs and refers to four biases plus a way of thinking as the set. The full visual content of those two graphics is available in the original PDF at `corpus.commons/demo/sources/original/lfuo-learning-review-guide-2024.pdf` for any reader who needs the precise lists.

Image classification was deferred — this was a text-only ingest; the `ingesting-images` skill can be applied later to classify diagrams and the Five Hows / Watch Outs graphics if they are needed downstream.

The PDF (3.5 MB) was promoted from `sources/ingest/` to `sources/original/` and is shipped in the open profile because the document is in the public domain under 17 USC §105 (US federal-employee work, hosted on a US Government S3 bucket, no copyright notice).
